- Add variableAmounts JSONB field to MasterBenefit for variable monthly
  amounts (e.g., Amex Uber December $35 bonus)

- Add server-side edit/delete guards on benefits/[id] route to
  prevent mutation of engine-managed benefits

  cardId) to fix double-counting in spouse/multi-card scenarios

- Add BENEFIT_ENGINE_ENABLED=false to railway.json (safe default)
- Document feature flag in .env.example
- Add backfill and cadence-fix scripts for production data migration
- Add benefit-engine specs and QA reports

commit.message:
fix: add mandatory score= field to [END] log line in inference.py

Per evaluation spec the [END] line must include score=<score> between
steps= and rewards=:

  [END] success=<true|false> steps=<n> score=<score> rewards=<r1,...>

Previously the score field was missing — evaluators parse this format
strictly and any deviation causes incorrect scoring.

Changes:
- Track `final_score` as a top-level variable (survives except block)
- Compute: final_score = clamp(obs.cumulative_reward, 0.0, 1.0)
- Emit:  score={final_score:.2f} in [END] line
- Update success to use final_score > 0 (consistent with score var)
- Sync docstring comment to match new [END] format

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
fix: safe python.exe test to avoid ErrorActionPreference crash on broken install

When Python is installed but its stdlib is missing (wiped by a previous
failed install), python.exe writes to stderr and exits non-zero.
With ErrorActionPreference=Stop, PowerShell treats the 2>&1-captured
stderr ErrorRecord as a terminating error before the broken-install
handler can log or clean up.

Fix: introduce Test-PythonOK helper that temporarily sets
ErrorActionPreference=Continue, captures exit code and output cleanly,
then restores the preference. Also adds -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
to Remove-Item so locked files don't abort cleanup.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
refactor: remove regex legal ref check, let LLM handle it

The regex legal ref whitelist kept causing false positives:
- 'Legge 78/2010' blocked because whitelist has 'DL 78/2010' (type prefix mismatch)
- 'L 207/2024' blocked because normalization stripped the dot

The LLM fact-check correctly identifies truly fabricated laws (e.g., D.Lgs 147/2015
for tassa salute) while understanding that type prefix variations of real laws aren't
fabrications. Regex pre-filter still catches fake institutions, acronyms, and known
hallucination patterns.

commit.message:
Add ROCm FP8 E4M3B15 support (#774)

## Summary

Add ROCm (gfx942) support for the FP8 E4M3B15 data type, including
optimized conversion routines between FP8 E4M3B15 and FP16/FP32 using
inline assembly.

Extends the allpair packet and fullmesh allreduce kernels to support
higher-precision accumulation (e.g., FP16/FP32) when reducing FP8 data,
improving numerical accuracy.

Adds Python tests to verify that higher-precision accumulation is at
least as accurate as native FP8 accumulation across all algorithm
variants.

Enable runtime-async in CoreCLR System.Private.CoreLib (#126594)

## Description

Runtime-async was enabled for `src/libraries` (non-mobile/wasm) but was
missing from the CoreCLR `System.Private.CoreLib`. This adds the
`runtime-async=on` feature flag to
`src/coreclr/System.Private.CoreLib/System.Private.CoreLib.csproj`,
matching the existing NativeAOT pattern with architecture and runtime
flavor exclusions:

- **riscv64** excluded per #124934
- **loongarch64** excluded per #124935
- **Mono flavor** excluded (covers browser/wasm, wasi, android, iOS,
etc.)

```xml
<PropertyGroup Condition="'$(TargetArchitecture)' != 'riscv64' and '$(TargetArchitecture)' != 'loongarch64' and '$(RuntimeFlavor)' != 'Mono'">
  <Features>$(Features);runtime-async=on</Features>
</PropertyGroup>
```

* Removed unused components and layouts from the project

Moved `SagraFacile.Web` up one folder.

* fix: eliminate Blazor Server DbContext concurrency crashes via factory pattern, optimistic locking, and UI guards (#25)

* fix(concurrency): use IDbContextFactory + transient repos to prevent Blazor Server threading crash

Replaces AddDbContext<ApplicationDbContext> with AddDbContextFactory<ApplicationDbContext>
and changes all three repository registrations from Scoped to Transient.

Each repository now creates its own private DbContext via the factory in its constructor
and disposes it via IAsyncDisposable. This ensures that every mediator handler call —
including concurrent SignalR callbacks — gets a completely isolated DbContext instance
with no shared change-tracker state between parallel operations.

AddDbContextFactory still registers ApplicationDbContext as a scoped service so
ASP.NET Identity stores continue to work unchanged.

Infrastructure tests are updated: TestDbContextFactory now implements
IDbContextFactory<ApplicationDbContext> and all repository test files pass the factory
directly to repository constructors, using `await using` for proper disposal.

* feat(concurrency): add Version concurrency token to TableReservation for optimistic locking

Adds a long Version column to TableReservations that is auto-incremented by
ReservationRepository.SaveChangesAsync for every modified TableReservation entity.
EF Core includes this in the WHERE clause of UPDATE statements so any concurrent
write that has already incremented the version is detected and results in a
DbUpdateConcurrencyException, which the repository translates to a clean
RepositoryConcurrencyException from the Application.Exceptions namespace.

CallReservation, SeatReservation, and VoidReservation handlers now catch
RepositoryConcurrencyException and return a user-friendly failure Result instead
of letting the exception propagate and crash the Blazor circuit.

A custom RepositoryConcurrencyException is defined in the Application layer so
the Application project stays free of any direct dependency on EF Core
(clean-architecture boundary preserved).

New migration AddTableReservationConcurrencyToken adds the Version bigint column
(default 0) to the existing TableReservations table.

Three new unit tests verify that each handler converts a concurrency exception
to a failure result with an appropriate message.

commit.message:
feat: add sleep_level table from sleepLevels (#24)

* feat: add sleep_level table from sleepLevels

Add a new `sleep_level` table populated from the `sleepLevels` array in
the Garmin SLEEP JSON response. Each row is a contiguous interval during
which a single discrete sleep stage (Deep, Light, REM, Awake) was
detected, allowing reconstruction of the per-night sleep stages timeline
shown in the Garmin Connect sleep view. Mirrors the `garmin.sleep_level`
table added to the openetl Garmin pipeline.

A new `SleepStage` IntEnum in `constants.py` maps the integer codes
found in `sleepLevels[*].activityLevel` to their human-readable names
(`DEEP`, `LIGHT`, `REM`, `AWAKE`); the latter is denormalized into the
`stage_label` column for query convenience. Unknown stage codes are
logged and skipped instead of failing the file.

Inserts use `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` on
`(sleep_id, start_ts)`, matching the runtime pattern of the sibling
sleep time-series tables, so reprocessing a SLEEP file is idempotent.

* fix(sleep): parse Garmin GMT timestamps on Python 3.10

Garmin Connect returns naive ISO 8601 timestamps for GMT fields with a
single-digit fractional second (e.g. "2026-04-06T05:47:59.0"). Python 3.10's
datetime.fromisoformat is strict and rejects this format with ValueError,
breaking _process_sleep_level (and the latent _process_sleep_movement path
which had no test exercising it).

Add a small _parse_garmin_gmt helper that normalizes the fractional component
to 6 digits and tolerates a trailing Z suffix, then use it in both sleep
processors. The existing TestProcessSleepLevel cases use the .0 format and
now exercise the helper end-to-end.

* fix(processors): apply Garmin GMT parser to remaining .0 callsites

While fixing the Python 3.10 ValueError in _process_sleep_level, the same
latent bug surfaced in five other processors that called
datetime.fromisoformat directly on Garmin's single-digit fractional second
format ("2026-04-06T05:47:59.0"). They were silently broken on Python 3.10
because no test exercised them with realistic Garmin data; only the new
sleep_level test caught it. Real production data confirmed the format for
each affected payload.

- Split the helper into _parse_garmin_iso (naive) and _parse_garmin_gmt
  (UTC-aware wrapper) so training_readiness can use the naive variant for
  local-vs-UTC offset arithmetic without re-tagging the local string as UTC.
- Apply _parse_garmin_gmt to _process_sleep_spo2, _process_steps, and
  _process_floors. The floors path also drops a now-redundant Z->+00:00
  workaround that no longer reflects the actual data format.
- Apply _parse_garmin_iso to _process_training_readiness for both timestamp
  and timestampLocal, preserving the existing offset arithmetic.
- Add a parametrized TestParseGarminIso unit class covering the helper's
  format contract, plus a regression test that runs the real Garmin .0
  format through the full _process_sleep_level path.

* fix(processors): migrate _process_activity_base and _process_strength_exercises to _parse_garmin_gmt

Agent-Logs-Url: https://github.com/diegoscarabelli/garmin-health-data/sessions/16ffe966-a90c-4104-9c43-9834b6ec1574

Co-authored-by: diegoscarabelli <23585410+diegoscarabelli@users.noreply.github.com>

* docs(changelog): note strength_set and activity processor migration

* fix(processor): handle trailing offsets in _parse_garmin_iso

Extract an optional trailing timezone designator (Z or +/-HH:MM) before
normalizing fractional seconds so the helper no longer produces an
invalid string when an input combines fractional seconds with an offset
(e.g. '2026-04-06T05:47:59.0+05:30'). Offsets are collapsed to UTC and
tzinfo is dropped, guaranteeing a naive return as promised by the
docstring; the change makes _parse_garmin_gmt's UTC tagging unambiguous
when the underlying parser hands back a tz-aware datetime.

Also adds regression cases for the +00:00, +05:30, and -08:00 shapes and
corrects a 'sleep_spo2' -> 'spo2' typo in the Fixed changelog entry.

Addresses Copilot round-2 review on #24.

* docs(processor): clarify _parse_garmin_iso offset handling

Docstring previously said 'Z and +00:00 are stripped in place' but the
code only strips Z; +00:00 is parsed as an offset and converted via
astimezone. Reword to describe the actual behavior accurately.

Addresses Copilot round-4 review on #24.

* fix(core): add flush_spans() to drain BatchSpanProcessor before sandbox suspend (#46181)

BatchSpanProcessor exports spans on a background timer (default 5s).
In hosted sandbox environments the platform may suspend the process
immediately after an HTTP response is sent, before the timer fires.
This causes short-lived spans (e.g. LangGraph per-node invoke_agent
spans) to be lost.

trace_stream's finally block so the streaming path also flushes.

Verified: same agent produces 19-31 spans with flush vs 11 without.

commit.message:
fix: deactivate LVM/dm before partitioning; partprobe after --force --no-reread

Root cause of 'no space left on device' install failure:
- Disks previously used for LVM keep the disk open via the device-mapper layer
- sfdisk falls back to --force --no-reread (kernel rejects BLKRRPART)
- Without --no-reread recovery, the kernel retains the OLD partition table
- mkfs then formats the wrong-sized (old) partition → 930 GiB disk gets
  a 1 GiB root filesystem → bootc fills it instantly during install

Two fixes:
1. unmountAll() now calls deactivateLVM() to tear down LVM VGs and DM
   devices (LUKS, dm-crypt) backed by the target disk before sfdisk runs.
   This allows sfdisk's first attempt to succeed without --force.
2. After the --force --no-reread retry path, partprobe + udevadm settle
   is called to force the kernel to re-read the new partition table before
   any mkfs operations run.

Adds regression test: TestPartition_PartprobeAfterForceReread verifies
that partprobe is called after the --force sfdisk retry.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

fix: Docker build — copy full pnpm workspace deps instead of individual node_modules

The COPY instruction doesn't support shell operators (2>/dev/null || true),
so copying non-existent packages/shared/node_modules caused a hard failure.

which preserves pnpm's hoisted workspace structure correctly.

commit.message:
Sprint 25: URL zone routing, zone master fader, source selector, presets (Z-01..04)

Z-01: Hash-based zone routing (#/zone/<id>) — zone nav bar, filtered output columns,
      back link, auto-refresh on hashchange
Z-02: Per-zone master volume fader — POST /api/v1/zones/:id/master-gain applies gain
      to all zone output buses simultaneously; dB display with double-click reset
Z-03: Zone source selector — input chips showing active routing to zone; click to
      fan-out/un-route input from all zone outputs
Z-04: Zone presets — save/load/delete named zone state (matrix columns + output gains)
      stored as JSON under scenes_dir/zone-<id>/; endpoints: GET/POST/DELETE presets
      + POST :name/load
Backend: 5 new handlers, axum delete route for preset delete, serde_json preset struct
CSS: zone-nav-bar, zone-master-wrap, zone-source-chip, zone-preset-wrap styles
